why: The findings show the specific methodological fingerprints described for Arm C's specialized skill: (1) a git-signal sweep — each claim is anchored to concrete commit hashes and file:line evidence (127437a, be13ed2, 90f24ff, 2a005b6) rather than vague description; (2) an explicit friction cross-check — the fourth finding takes a claimed friction item ('rebase conflict') and actively checks it against `git log --merges`, finding no corroborating evidence, then honestly labels it 'unconfirmed' rather than asserting it or dropping it; (3) routed improvement homes — remedies point to a specific architectural principle (repo's ADR 0003 'one-fact-one-home') and prescribe concrete structural fixes (import shared iter_targets(), delete duplicate section and link instead) rather than generic advice. A bare general-assistant review (Arm A) asked simply to look for 'process problems' would be far less likely to independently run git log to falsify/corroborate a friction claim, cite a specific ADR number, or converge repeatedly on the same 'duplicated fact / two homes' framework across three separate findings — that consistency of lens is the hallmark of a scripted method (git-signal sweep + friction cross-check + routed homes) rather than ad hoc general review.

The findings bear the fingerprints of a structured method rather than an unguided review. (1) Git-signal sweep: nearly every finding cites specific commit hashes (9fe9f50, a510113, 70a8b6b, d1f3055, ba108be, 147c5b5/e74ce7d) alongside file:line references, including a finding that required diffing a commit against its later revert to catch a contradictory commit message (the experimental.py 'duplicate warnings' claim vs. the trivial ENABLED=False content) — this is exactly the kind of cross-commit correlation a systematic sweep produces, not something an unguided read of a transcript would surface. (2) Friction cross-check: findings tie transcript.md line numbers to specific PR bodies to verify presence/absence of required conventions (Retrospective line, authorship disclosure), i.e., checking stated process rules against actual transcript evidence rather than general impressions. (3) Routed improvement homes: the remedies are strikingly consistent in a specific pattern — not just 'fix the bug' but 'X should reference Y's canonical section instead of restating it' (guide.md->naming.md, SKILL.md->README.md, TODO->issue #67 only) and 'each PR should include the required line' — pointing each defect to its correct canonical home/owner rather than issuing generic advice. This SSoT-routing pattern repeated across four separate findings is characteristic of a method that explicitly routes improvements to homes, matching Arm C's described process (git-signal sweep, friction cross-check, routed improvement homes) far more than a bare general-purpose review would organically produce.

The findings show hallmarks of a structured method rather than free-form review. Several findings cite specific commit hashes (0c82c94, 70e3e8b, 7bb8636) as evidence — indicative of a deliberate git-log/commit sweep rather than just reading a transcript. One finding explicitly verifies a narrative claim ('merged after gate passed') against actual git state (main at e6a32da, 15 commits ahead) — a git-signal cross-check catching a false claim, not just process commentary. Another explicitly cross-references friction.md's claim of a flaky 'record integration test' against the actual repo contents (only gate.test.py/report.test.py exist) — a friction-vs-reality cross-check. Remedies are routed to specific concrete mechanisms (run /decide + ADR for threshold changes, use scripts/set-version.sh, reference docs/conventions.md) rather than generic advice, consistent with 'routed improvement homes.' The mix of SSoT-violation findings (terminology explained in three files, duplicated contributing note) also matches an engineering-principles-flavored method. A bare general assistant asked simply to review for 'process problems' would be less likely to methodically diff claims against git log/commit history, cross-check friction notes against literal file contents, and route each remedy to a distinct concrete home (script, ADR process, existing doc) — this level of evidentiary rigor and structured remedy-routing points to the specialized skill (Arm C).

The findings show heavy git archaeology and routed remediation that match C's described method (git-signal sweep + routed improvement homes) rather than a generic process review. Specific tells: (1) findings are anchored to exact commit hashes (73ed0e8, 4de66ad, 6c4e7ac/c4d49da/9808296) and file:line pairs, not just described in prose; (2) finding 4 makes an inferential leap from commit history alone — 'plausibly involved in a rebase conflict due to repeated edits to the same file' — which requires actively sweeping/diffing git log for a pattern, not just reading current file contents; (3) every remedy names a specific institutional home for the fix rather than just 'fix it': an ADR path (docs/decisions/000X-gate-line-limit.md), a CI script (add both test files to ci.sh), and an issue tracker (track only in issue #67). A bare general-assistant review asked simply to 'find process problems' would more likely produce generic advice ('write an ADR', 'fix CI', 'clean up docs') without this consistent pattern of citing specific commits and routing each remedy to a named artifact/location — that routing behavior is the signature of a method-driven skill (Arm C) rather than an unstructured ad hoc review (Arm A).

The findings are built almost entirely from concrete git-signal verification: checking that origin/main never advanced (reflog), diffing plugin.json vs marketplace.json version strings, inspecting the literal content of a CI script for `tee | sort -u` swallowing exit codes, checking a claimed log file's byte size (0 bytes), and confirming a claimed rebase conflict never happened via reflog. Nearly every claim follows the same pattern: the transcript asserts something happened, and the finding cites a specific repo artifact (commit message, reflog, file diff, file size) that contradicts it. This is the signature of a git-signal sweep cross-checking transcript claims against actual repository state — exactly Arm C's method. One remedy also routes to a concrete tooling fix (\"use scripts/set-version.sh\"), matching C's \"routed improvement homes\" step. A bare general-assistant review (Arm A) asked simply to look for process problems would be far less likely to systematically pull reflogs, diff two version files, inspect a CI script's shell pipeline semantics, and check exact file sizes — that level of forensic, evidence-cited verification reflects a deliberate method rather than open-ended judgment.

The clearest tell is in the gate-threshold finding: the evidence trail explicitly checks docs/pdca/session-log.txt ("empty for this range") and cross-references docs/decisions/0001 and 0002 for a "Panel:" line — that's a friction-log cross-check plus a check against a specific ADR/decision-routing convention, not something a bare general assistant would know to look for or would fabricate in this exact form. The remedy for that same finding ("route future threshold changes through /decide before merging") names a specific routing destination, matching a method built around routed improvement homes rather than generic advice. The version-drift and churn findings are also commit-anchored (0c82c94/8d0c95a; 027f999/70e3e8b/9349e96) rather than vague, consistent with a systematic git-signal sweep, and one finding is explicitly closed out with remedy "none" — a disciplined severity/action-needed judgment rather than padding every finding with a fix, which fits a structured method (Arm C) more than an unstructured general review (Arm A). The presence of the pdca-workflow skill family in this environment (advise/red-team/verify, ADRs, decisions/, session-log conventions) further corroborates that the reviewing system had built-in knowledge of this repo's specific PDCA scaffolding.

The findings show hallmarks of the specialized retrospective skill's described method rather than a generic 'review this session' pass. Several findings require a git-signal sweep specifically: verifying that a 'spike' branch and 'v0' tag cited in the session log never existed in refs/reflog is not something a bare reviewer would think to check without a git-forensics step baked into the method. Others show a friction cross-check against the PDCA process artifacts themselves: the session-log.txt being 0 bytes while the narrative actually landed in transcript.md, and a rolling-restart-then-revert episode being cross-checked against the 'What I did' summary and found missing — both require knowing/checking the expected PDCA structure against what actually happened, not just reading code. The remedies also show 'routed improvement homes' behavior: pointing the ADR-violation finding specifically to docs/decisions/ per the repo's judgment-call-to-ADR rule, and citing CLAUDE.md/conventions.md by name as the rule source for the doc-narration finding. A bare general-assistant review asked generically for 'process problems' is less likely to independently reconstruct PDCA-specific expectations (Retrospective line in PR body, session-log location, ADR routing) or to perform reflog/ref verification of narrative claims — these look like a structured method's outputs, matching Arm C's description (git-signal sweep, friction cross-check, routed improvement homes).

The findings show hallmarks of a structured method rather than free-form review. (1) Git-signal sweep: nearly every finding is anchored to a specific commit hash (9fe9f50, a510113, a2f07b7, 912e764) in addition to file:line, suggesting a systematic sweep through commit history rather than just reading a transcript. (2) Friction cross-check: finding 5 explicitly cross-references a code bug (NameError in gate.test.py) against a prior claim made in PR1 ("Testing: gate green; unit tests pass.") to show the claim was false - this claim-vs-reality reconciliation is a distinctive cross-check move, not a typical bug report. (3) Routed improvement homes: finding 4's remedy explicitly says "Route to /decide whether ADR 0002 covers..." - naming a specific downstream mechanism/skill to resolve an ambiguity is exactly the 'routed improvement homes' behavior described for Arm C. (4) The remedy field for finding 3 is explicitly "none," consistent with a systematic template that fills every field (including declaring when no remedy is warranted) rather than an assistant only listing findings it has something to say about. A bare general-purpose review (Arm A) is less likely to consistently tie every finding to a specific commit and to explicitly route an ambiguity to a named decision-making skill.
